text.heading: Living healthy during this Ramadan
text.p: Ramadan is a month when Muslim faithful fast and learn to exercise self-control. On the other hand, well balanced food is the key to healthy leaving.
Fasting generally, apart from its spiritual obligation, is good for one’s health. Abstaining from food and water for a period of time makes a person more physically fit and mentally alert.

In order to remain hale and hearty throughout Ramadan, normal quantities of food from the major food groups, such as bread, cereal, milk and dairy products, fish, meat, poultry, beans, vegetable and fruits should be eaten.

Sufficient water and juices should be taken during Iftar (Saari) and then between Sahur (breaking time) and Iftar to avoid dehydration. Intake of large amounts of caffeine-based beverages should be avoided, especially at Sahur, because this could cause insomnia.

Also, drinking too much tea during Sahur, will make one pass more urine and inescapably cause the loss of valuable mineral salts that your body would otherwise need during the day. Try as much as possible to drink and eat fruits at the beginning and end of each meal, especially at Sahur

p.italics: For overweight people, this is also an opportunity to get rid of excess weight/fat.

text.heading: - Gingered apple cider juice

p.italics: Process through a juicer and serve.

text.heading: - Mango orange apple juice

p.italics: Peel the orange, core the apple and cut all of the fruit into sections. Juice and enjoy.

text.heading: - Vibrant cocktail

p.italics: Peel the pineapple and juice everything and serve

text.heading: - Tropical juice flair

p.italics: Remove the skin of the pineapple and cut into pieces. Peel the mango and cut into spears. Juice the pineapple and mango pieces with the tomato. Place the juice in a sealable container, shake the juice thoroughly and pour into glasses
